summary: All round-1 findings are resolved. Test 6 is now a real wiring test: it constructs a Robolectric-backed DefaultModelRegistry, mutates `_models` through the new `@VisibleForTesting internal` accessor, and observes `registry.activeModelName` — a regression that replaced the override with `MutableStateFlow(hardcoded)` or aliased it to `models` would fail. The new mixed-list test pins the `firstOrNull { Ready }` predicate with three distinct modelIds across three scenarios. The tautology assertion is gone, test 5 renamed to match what it proves, and KDoc references in production code now point at Decision 7 rather than task numbers. The writer-loop race-defeat mechanism is the one new surface worth scrutinising — it works, but its flakiness margin and lack of registry-scope teardown are minor hygiene concerns, not blockers.

findings.issue: The constructed `DefaultModelRegistry` owns a `CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.Default)` (line 108 of DefaultModelRegistry.kt) that launches the init-block scan and is never cancelled when the test finishes. After this test returns, the scan coroutine keeps running on Robolectric's background threads alongside any follow-up test in the same JVM. In a single-test file this is contained, but (a) Robolectric reuses the JVM across classes, so leaked coroutines from this test can collide with unrelated tests later in the suite, and (b) the pattern will be copied to future registry wiring tests where the problem compounds. It is also the reason `runBlocking` had to be used instead of `runTest` — the registry's scope is independent of the test dispatcher, so nothing else would have worked.
findings.recommendation: Expose `scope` as `@VisibleForTesting internal` (same pattern just used for `_models`) and cancel it in a `@After` method — or, if avoiding additional visibility exposure is preferred, skip the scope surface and instead give the wiring test a tearDown that explicitly nulls out any static state and lets GC reclaim the registry. Minimum acceptable fix: add `@After fun tearDown() { /* any cleanup reachable without new visibility */ }` and document in a comment that the registry's scope intentionally outlives the test because no shutdown handle is exposed. Do not block merge on this — flag it for the follow-up hygiene pass referenced in round 1.

replacement.approach: Either (1) mark DefaultModelRegistry.scope as @VisibleForTesting internal and cancel in @After, or (2) leave as-is with an explanatory comment and commit to addressing once a real shutdown path exists (e.g. when a `close()` method is added to ModelRegistry in a later phase task).

findings.issue: The writer loop at `delay(5)` racing a 5-second `withTimeout` is very generous and will virtually never flake on developer machines, but the mechanism relies on the assumption that the init-block scan (`refreshAllowlist` + `scanLocalFiles` + `resumePartialDownloads` on `Dispatchers.Default`) is bounded. If a future change makes any of those suspend indefinitely (e.g. a network-backed allowlist, a flaky filesystem probe), the loop keeps overwriting and the test still passes — but if the scan path starts emitting `_models.update { ... list not containing probe ... }` faster than 5ms, the `first { it == probeHfId }` read could theoretically miss every emission. StateFlow only delivers the latest value to a late collector, so `first { it == probeHfId }` is correct: as long as at some point the probe value is the `.value`, the collector observes it. This is not actually flaky in the StateFlow semantics, but the test's comment (`Writer loop defeats the init-block scan race`) overstates the mechanism — the real defence is that `first { p }` on a StateFlow samples `.value` on every emission, not that the writer always wins.
findings.recommendation: Tighten the inline comment to reflect the actual mechanism: 'Writer loop keeps re-installing the probe entry so StateFlow.value lands on the probe at least once after every scan-induced overwrite — `first { it == probeHfId }` then observes that emission.' Alternatively, replace the loop with a single `_models.value = listOf(readyEntry)` write followed by `first { it == probeHfId }` — if this passes on CI consistently, the loop is unnecessary and the test becomes easier to reason about. (The loop is only needed if the scan keeps emitting after our single write; the write ordering is: scan fires on Dispatchers.Default, test thread writes, StateFlow takes the last write. A race is possible but bounded by how long the scan's `update { }` lambda holds the StateFlow.) Try the single-write variant on CI before committing to the loop.

replacement.approach: Rephrase the `Writer loop defeats the init-block scan race` comment to describe the StateFlow.first sampling semantics. Optionally experiment with a single write to see if the loop is needed.

taskRequired.rationale: All round-1 findings (1 major, 3 minor, 2 nit) are resolved cleanly. The major — test 6 wiring gap — is fully closed: mutating `_models` directly on a real `DefaultModelRegistry` and asserting that `activeModelName` reflects the change catches a hardcoded-override regression that the helper-only tests could not. The two new minors flagged here (registry scope leak; writer-loop comment) are pure hygiene — they do not affect correctness, regression coverage, or build determinism — and can roll into the hygiene pass mentioned in round 1 or be deferred entirely. No task required.
